Author: Addy Osmani
Title: Optimizing LLM Coding Workflow for 2026 | Addy Osmani posted on the topic | LinkedIn
Text:
"My LLM coding workflow going into 2026" My latest free write-up: https://lnkd.in/grS9Zryf ✍ AI coding assistants (Cursor, Claude Code, Gemini CLI and friends) have shifted from novelty to daily drivers, but harnessing them effectively requires skill, structure, and discipline. At Anthropic, engineers use Claude Code so heavily that ~90% of the code for the tool is written by the tool itself - I doubt many of us are quite seeing that level of gain, but getting that result requires a shift in mindset: Treat the LLM as a powerful pair programmer that requires clear direction, context and oversight rather than autonomous judgment. After a year of refining my "AI-assisted engineering" process, here is the playbook I'm taking into 2026: 1. Specs before code (The "15-minute waterfall") Don't just throw wishes at the prompt. I start by brainstorming a detailed spec.md with the AI - defining requirements, data models, and edge cases before a single line of code is written. 2. Scope management is everything Avoid monolithic outputs. Break work into iterative steps. If you ask for the whole app, you get a "jumbled mess." If you ask for one function, you get precision. 3. Context Packing LLMs are only as good as the information you feed them. I explicitly "dump" relevant code, docs, and constraints into the context window. and use MCPs that make this easier. Don't make the AI guess. 4. Git is your Save Point I commit significantly more often now. Treat commits as "save points in a game" - if the AI hallucinates or breaks the build, you need a clean state to roll back to immediately. 5. Trust but verify (The human loop) My cardinal rule: Never blindly trust the output. AI is over-confident and prone to mistakes. You must review, run, and test every snippet. I often use a second AI model just to review the code generated by the first. MCPs like Chrome DevTools MCP can also help with your debugging and quality loop. The bottom line: AI coding tools are incredible force multipliers, but they do not replace the need for engineering fundamentals. In 2026, the human engineer remains the director of the show. Read the full breakdown of my tools, prompts, and testing strategies in the article above. 👆 #ai #programming #softwareengineering #lifeatgoogle
Really solid breakdown. Treating LLMs as disciplined pair programmers — not autonomous devs — is the key shift. Specs first, tight scope, strong context, frequent commits, and always a human review loop. AI multiplies output, but fundamentals still win. Great share.
Addy Osmani This resonates. The real unlock isn’t better prompts, it’s a better process. Treating LLMs as force multipliers inside disciplined engineering loops, not autonomous agents, is what separates leverage from fragility. Specs, scope control, and human review feel increasingly like the new “senior engineering” skills.
The 'specs before code' point is something I keep coming back to. Claude is fastest when the problem is well-defined, not when you're vague and hoping it guesses right. Git as save points is practical too - I do this constantly since rollback beats debate about whose version was better.
